text: The robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy is performed using the da Vinci SI system with four arms. The left robotic arm holds a bipolar instrument for grasping, while the right arm uses scissors for dissection. An assistant uses an additional port to tent the peritoneum, providing counter-traction to facilitate the dissection process.

The surgeon performs a robotic prostatectomy using a transperitoneal approach, accessing the prostate through the peritoneum. The posterior approach is utilized, allowing for the dissection and cutting of the vas deferens, followed by the dissection of the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon continues the robotic-assisted transperitoneal prostatectomy, using bipolar cautery set at thirty to precisely dissect around the freed vas deferens and locate the right and left seminal vesicles. The dissection minimizes monopolar cautery to protect surrounding nerve and vascular tissue, employing blunt techniques near the nerve tips adjacent to the seminal vesicles.

The surgeon dissects through Denonvilliers' fascia, lifting both seminal vesicles to enhance exposure. Cold water irrigation is used to keep the surgical field clear and cool, aiding in nerve dissection and reducing thermal energy spread. The dissection progresses towards the apex, creating a working space for subsequent bladder procedures.

The surgeon uses sharp and blunt dissection to open the extraperitoneal space, pulling on the medial umbilical ligament with a grasping instrument. A visible line of dissection appears, aiding in exposing the space. The endopelvic fascia and pubic symphysis become visible, with the prostate located just below the bipolar instrument. The dissection proceeds towards the superficial dorsal vein and puboprostatics on the right side of the prostate, before returning to the camera port to locate the uracus.

The surgeon begins defatting the prostate, noting minimal fat overlying the structure, which aids in better exposure and easier management of potential bleeding. The right endopelvic fascia is opened, revealing the levator musculature adjacent to the scissors. Cold irrigation is used to maintain tissue exposure, with bipolar cautery available to minimize heat spread. The bladder dissection has commenced, but the dorsal vein complex remains intact to preserve surrounding neurovascular tissue.

The surgeon exposes the Foley catheter, using the fourth robotic arm to hold it against the pelvic sidewall. The posterior bladder neck dissection is underway, with careful attention to avoid rectal injury and maintain proper bladder wall thickness. The assistant uses suction to manage bleeding and maintain traction on the posterior bladder wall, revealing the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The surgeon proceeds with nerve-sparing techniques, employing both retrograde and anti-grade approaches to preserve vascular tissue while avoiding the prostate.

The surgeon begins the anti-grade nerve sparing technique, focusing on securing the left vascular pedicle of the prostate. The surgical team works within a previously created space, carefully isolating the vascular pedicle while avoiding the neurovascular bundle. Large Hem-o-lok clips are applied to secure the pedicle, and additional clips or bipolar cautery may be used to prevent back bleeding.

The surgeon navigates through the vascular pedicle, setting up for nerve sparing near the apex of the prostate. Minor bleeding occurs from attachments on the prostate's underbelly, managed with pinpoint cautery while the assistant irrigates the area. The dorsal vein complex remains unsecured, with minimal bleeding observed. The neuromuscular bundle on the left side is healthy, and cuts are made to approach the prostate apex. The surgeon considers using clips or cautery instead of sutures for the dorsal vein complex. The procedure shifts to the right side, where the fourth robotic arm complicates movement, but the pedicle is secured on the left side, allowing for conventional retrograde nerve sparing.

The surgeon uses a CT-1 needle with 2-0 PDS suture to secure the dorsal vein, tying a slip knot to minimize bleeding. After placing the stitch, another stitch is anchored to the pubic symphysis to help replicate the urethrovesical angle post-prostate removal.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection around the apex of the prostate, using pinpoint bipolar cautery to ensure hemostatic control and preserve the urethral sphincter while maximizing urethral length. The neurovascular bundles are visible, merging underneath the prostate, and the posterior wall is preserved to avoid tension on the neurovascular bundle. Flow seal is applied to achieve hemostasis, followed by pressure application to ensure effectiveness before resuming the urethrovesical anastomosis using monocryl sutures.

The surgeon observes clumps of FloSeal in the surgical field and gently irrigates to reduce the granules. On the left side, a clear suture is used, while a colored suture is used on the other side to avoid confusion during the procedure. Before connecting the bladder to the urethra, an amniotic wrap is applied to prevent scarring and aid in recovery of urinary and sexual function. The wrap is made from amniotic tissue and is applied as part of the nerve-sparing technique.

The surgeon performs the anastomosis, ensuring both instruments are visible to prevent suture breakage. After completing the anastomosis, the bladder is filled with 120 cc of fluid to test for leaks. Minimal bleeding is observed, and the pneumoperitoneum is adjusted to optimize patient recovery. A drain is placed, concluding the procedure.
